The literal meaning of "silent" (making no sound) and "treatment" (medical care or behavior towards someone) does not fully capture the intended meaning in this context. Therefore, "silent treatment" is used idiomatically here.

sentence: Jay Branegan says each side will be preaching to the choir. "Democrats will make the argument that's been successful with their base . . . [that] Republicans are merely helping the rich."

sentence: After having shown us three hours of instructional and safety videos, the inspector was simply beating a dead horse by telling us to buckle up as we got into the van.

args.explanation: The expression "beating a dead horse" is an idiom. It means to waste effort on something that cannot succeed or be changed, or to continue talking about a subject that has already been decided or is no longer relevant. In this sentence, the inspector is telling the passengers to buckle up after they have already watched safety videos and are getting into the van. The safety message about buckling up has already been conveyed through the videos, and repeating it at this point is redundant and unlikely to have any further effect, hence it is figuratively "beating a dead horse."
